impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in granting JMOL that Teva's headache patent claims covering humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies for treating headache were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's JMOL of invalidity for Teva's patents claiming methods of using humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies to treat headache, after a jury had found willful infringement and no invalidity. The court remanded for further proceedings, reinstating the jury's findings. This case involves blockbuster migraine drugs (Teva's Ajovy vs. Lilly's competing product) and has significant implications for the scope of written-description and enablement requirements for biologic drug patents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Strengthens patent protection for biologic drug innovators by preserving broader genus claims covering humanized antibodies, supporting the ability of pharmaceutical companies to enforce patents against competitors in the lucrative migraine treatment market.
impacts.negativeImplications: Increases risk for companies developing biosimilar or competing biologic therapies, as broader patent claims may survive validity challenges, potentially delaying market entry and competition.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder that conditions patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of its unpatented products engages in unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the unpatented products had substantial non-infringing uses that would preclude antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld antitrust liability and the corresponding damages award, finding sufficient evidence that the tied products had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent's scope.
impacts.positiveImplications: Reinforces the ability of competitors to challenge anticompetitive patent licensing practices that extend monopoly power into unpatented product markets, promoting fair competition.
impacts.negativeImplications: Creates significant risk for patent holders that bundle patent licenses with requirements to purchase unpatented products, as such arrangements may constitute unlawful tying even where the patented and unpatented products are in related markets.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls made in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are abstract under step one of the Alice framework, and whether damages may be based on foreign sales of infringing cybersecurity software.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the judgment in a $185M cybersecurity patent case, holding that Columbia's claims for detecting anomalous program executions were abstract at Alice step one and required further step-two analysis on remand. The court also held that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. The case has major implications for software patent eligibility and extraterritorial damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Provides clarity for technology companies defending against software patent assertions by reinforcing Alice eligibility standards for computer security patents and limiting damages exposure from foreign sales.
impacts.negativeImplications: Universities and other patent holders face heightened risk that cybersecurity and software method patents will be found abstract under Alice, and cannot rely on worldwide sales to calculate damages.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods and systems for server-side tailoring of content specifications to wireless device capabilities were ineligible for patenting under § 101, reversing a jury verdict of infringement and $2.5M in damages. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content to device characteristics. This decision reinforces limits on patentability for content delivery technology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Benefits major streaming and technology companies by invalidating broad patents covering fundamental content-adaptation concepts, reducing exposure to infringement claims from non-practicing entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in streaming media and content delivery face increased difficulty in enforcing patents covering server-side content optimization, as such claims may be found abstract under § 101.

impacts.caseName: International Medical Devices, Inc. v. Cornell
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the plaintiff established that each of its four asserted trade secrets was a protectable trade secret under California law, and whether generally known information can constitute an inventive contribution for patent inventorship purposes.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the jury's finding of trade secret misappropriation, holding there was insufficient evidence that the asserted trade secrets regarding cosmetic penile implants were protectable under California law because the information was generally known. The court also reversed the patent invalidity finding because the purported inventive contribution consisted of generally known ideas. However, the court affirmed liability on a counterfeiting claim.
impacts.positiveImplications: Provides important protection for businesses accused of trade secret misappropriation by reinforcing that plaintiffs must prove each asserted trade secret is not generally known, raising the evidentiary bar for trade secret claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on trade secret protection face increased scrutiny on whether their claimed secrets are truly protectable rather than generally known information in their industry.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether U.S. patent claims covering multicore processor operations are infringed when the accused products are designed domestically but manufactured and sold abroad, and whether the district court erred in striking plaintiff's damages expert theories.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit addressed extraterritoriality and doctrine of equivalents issues in a patent dispute between VLSI and Intel over multicore processor technology. The court reversed in part the grant of summary judgment of noninfringement, finding genuine disputes of material fact, and remanded for further proceedings. The decision addresses important questions about the territorial reach of U.S. patents for semiconductor designs.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders may pursue infringement claims even where accused products are manufactured abroad, provided there is sufficient domestic conduct, expanding the reach of U.S. patent enforcement for globally manufactured technology.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies with global manufacturing operations face increased patent infringement exposure for products designed in the U.S. but manufactured overseas.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative testimony is admissible at summary judgment even when the witness lacks personal knowledge, and whether the on-sale bar under 35 U.S.C. § 102(b) requires that a prior art sale explicitly disclose the device's functionality.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment invalidating patent claims under the on-sale bar, holding that Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative testimony is admissible even when the witness lacks personal knowledge, and that the on-sale bar does not require that prior sales explicitly disclose the device's functionality. The court also addressed admissibility of source code as non-hearsay evidence.
impacts.positiveImplications: Strengthens defendants' ability to prove the on-sale bar at summary judgment by permitting corporate representative testimony without personal knowledge and by not requiring proof that prior sales disclosed functionality.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face broader on-sale bar challenges, as prior commercial sales of devices need not have disclosed the claimed functionality to qualify as invalidating prior art.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly determined that Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen measurement functionality infringed Masimo's patents and that Masimo satisfied the domestic industry requirement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing Apple Watch models that infringed Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement technology, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation. The court found no error in the domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings. This high-profile case affects Apple's ability to sell its flagship wearable product.
impacts.positiveImplications: Validates the ITC as an effective enforcement mechanism for patent holders against major technology companies, supporting the ability to obtain import exclusion orders for infringing consumer electronics.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major technology companies face continued risk of import bans through ITC proceedings, even for popular consumer products, when incorporating patented technology from competitors.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim for attempted monopolization.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments on both inequitable conduct (granted in favor of Global Tubing) and Walker Process fraud (granted in favor of Tenaris), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves patents for quench-and-tempered coiled tubing used in oil and gas, where Tenaris allegedly withheld prior art documents acquired from a predecessor company during patent prosecution.
impacts.positiveImplications: Provides defendants in patent cases greater opportunity to present inequitable conduct defenses at trial, while also protecting patent holders from premature Walker Process antitrust liability.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that acquire prior art through corporate acquisitions face increased scrutiny regarding disclosure obligations during patent prosecution, and may face both unenforceability and antitrust liability if material references are withheld.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action when it missed the deadline for seeking a stay of the original action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that an ITC respondent cannot circumvent the statutory deadline for obtaining a mandatory stay of a district court action by voluntarily dismissing and refiling the action to reset the clock under § 1659(a)(2). This decision prevents strategic manipulation of parallel ITC and district court proceedings in pharmaceutical patent disputes between competing drug manufacturers.
impacts.positiveImplications: Protects declaratory judgment plaintiffs from delay tactics by ITC respondents who seek to stay district court proceedings through procedural gamesmanship, preserving access to timely judicial resolution of patent disputes.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the statutory deadline for a mandatory stay cannot cure the deficiency by refiling, limiting their strategic options for managing parallel proceedings.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed claims relating to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the challenged claims would have been obvious over the cited prior art under the correct construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction in an IPR involving DivX's patent on streaming encrypted media content, vacated the Board's decision that Netflix failed to prove obviousness, and remanded for further proceedings. The decision affects how encryption information limitations are construed in streaming media patents, with implications for the digital content delivery industry.
impacts.positiveImplications: Benefits streaming technology companies by enabling broader prior art challenges to patents on encrypted content delivery through corrected claim construction at the PTAB.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital media streaming space face renewed obviousness challenges when claim construction is corrected to potentially broader scope.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report in a patent case against Microsoft's Azure platform, vacating summary judgment based on absence of a remedy. The court held the district court applied an overly stringent standard in excluding the expert's opinions on damages for infringement of networking optimization patents, restoring the plaintiff's opportunity to prove damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Provides patent plaintiffs greater latitude in presenting damages evidence, lowering the risk that meritorious infringement claims will be dismissed solely due to exclusion of expert testimony.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies defending against patent claims may face broader admissibility of damages expert testimony, increasing trial exposure and settlement pressure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing PTAB discretionary denial of IPR petitions (including Fintiv-type rules) are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b). The court held the instructions do not bind the Director and relate to unreviewable discretionary authority, meaning they need not undergo formal rulemaking procedures.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the Director's ability to quickly adjust IPR institution policies without formal rulemaking, potentially reducing duplicative proceedings when parallel litigation exists.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and patent challengers face continued uncertainty about IPR institution policies that can change without public notice and comment, potentially limiting access to IPR as a tool for challenging patents.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were permissible.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary exceeded its statutory authority by charging PACER fees to fund unrelated expenses. The court upheld attorneys' fees, costs, and incentive awards to the three nonprofit class representatives, finding the settlement fair and the objector's challenges meritless. The decision affects hundreds of thousands of PACER users.
impacts.positiveImplications: Validates the class action mechanism for recovering overcharges from government fee programs and confirms that incentive awards to class representatives remain permissible, encouraging future class actions.
